metadata.title: Retrieval Practice: Quizzing that Builds Memory
metadata.summary: A ~12-minute primer on retrieval practice (the 'testing effect') — why recalling beats re-reading — with a knowledge check and a classroom action plan. General educator PD; no AlloFlow features required.

activities.title: What retrieval practice is — and why it works

content.body:
Retrieval practice means having learners bring information to mind from memory — answering a question, writing what they remember, explaining an idea aloud — rather than reviewing it again. The act of retrieving strengthens memory more than re-reading or re-watching the same material. Researchers call this the 'testing effect.'

This is one of the most consistently replicated findings in learning science (e.g., Roediger & Karpicke, 2006, and many studies since), and it holds across ages, subjects, and formats. The benefit is largest when retrieval is effortful but successful, and when it is spaced out over time rather than crammed.

Important: the goal is LEARNING, not grading. Retrieval works best when it is low- or no-stakes — frequent, ungraded or lightly weighted practice — which gets the memory benefit while keeping anxiety low. High-stakes, infrequent testing does not.
keyPoints: Recalling from memory builds retention more than re-reading.
It is well-replicated across ages and subjects (testing effect).
Keep it low-/no-stakes and spaced over time.

questions.prompt: The 'testing effect' (retrieval practice) is best described as:
options: Re-reading highlighted notes is the most efficient way to study
Recalling information from memory strengthens later retention more than re-reading it
Frequent graded tests mainly serve accountability
Matching instruction to each student's learning style

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest example of retrieval practice?
options: Re-reading the chapter and highlighting key sentences
Watching a recap video of the lesson
A brief, closed-notes 'brain dump' where students write everything they remember

questions.prompt: To get the memory benefit while keeping anxiety low, retrieval practice should usually be:
options: Replaced by re-reading for struggling students
Saved only for the end-of-year exam
Low- or no-stakes (ungraded or lightly weighted)

questions.prompt: Spacing retrieval practice across days and weeks (instead of cramming it) tends to:

activities.title: Pick retrieval routines you'll commit to

items: Open a lesson with a 2-minute 'what do you remember from last time?' brain dump.
End class with 3–5 ungraded recall questions.
Use short, low-stakes quizzes spaced across the unit — not only before the test.
Have students close their notes and explain a concept from memory before reviewing it.

content.prompt: Name one lesson you will teach in the next two weeks. Which ONE retrieval routine will you add, and at what moment in the lesson? Write it here.
